steps.title: Spot the confirmation pattern
steps.content: The first sentence claims the installation creates a “whimsical yet perplexing experience.” The second sentence shows this effect in action: visitors are “dazzled and confused.” The transition word must signal that this proves the claim.

steps.title: Apply the signal to the choices
steps.content:
The logical relationship is confirmation: the second sentence proves the first sentence's claim by showing visitors are actually "dazzled and confused." The blank needs a transition word signaling that the following evidence confirms the preceding claim.
  • Choice A — ‘Second,’: signals a second item in an enumeration, so it fails because the passage isn't listing steps. ✘
  • Choice B — ‘Nevertheless,’: signals contrast despite an obstacle, so it fails because the second sentence supports, not opposes, the first. ✘
  • Choice C — ‘Indeed,’: signals that the following evidence confirms the preceding claim, so it fits perfectly—visitors being dazzled and confused proves the "whimsical yet perplexing" claim. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘Instead,’: signals a replacement or substitution, so it fails because there is no alternative being offered. ✘
That confirms C is correct.
